nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of sheltered lagoons and inshore coral reefs, typically at 1–16 m depth; has been documented occurring in small groups on Acropora patches

decorPreferences: Acropora or similar branching coral structure

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on zooplankton and algae in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied diet including mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, small frozen meaty foods, spirulina-based preparations, and high-quality flake or pellet food. Multiple small feedings per day tend to be preferred over a single large meal.

summary: A hardy, vividly colored Indo-Pacific damselfish with a bright blue body and striking yellow tail. Generally considered one of the more tractable damselfish and is commonly kept in reef aquariums; tends toward semi-aggressive behavior, especially toward conspecifics in smaller tanks.
careNotes:
The Yellowtail Damselfish is widely regarded as one of the hardier and more manageable members of the damselfish family, and is often recommended for beginners. It tends toward semi-aggressive behavior — LiveAquaria describes it as semi-aggressive while noting territorial tendencies toward conspecifics; SaltwaterAquariumBlog rates it outright aggressive and notes incompatibility with clownfish; ReefApp describes it as peaceful within the genus. The middle assessment of semi-aggressive is used here. A single specimen or a small odd-numbered group (3, 5) is generally advised; odd groupings have been reported to diffuse aggression relative to pairs.

This species is an Indo-Pacific reef fish (Western Pacific: Philippines, Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands, Ryukyu Islands) and should not be confused with Microspathodon chrysurus (the Caribbean Jewel Damselfish), which is sometimes sold under the trade name 'Yellowtail Damselfish' in the Atlantic trade.

Temperature: Hobbyist sources (LiveAquaria, SaltwaterAquariumBlog) generally cite 72–80°F (22.2–26.7°C), while FishBase ecological modeling gives 27.3–29.3°C. The values here are averaged across all consulted sources (min 23.5°C, max 27.0°C); the FishBase ecological model likely reflects warmer in-situ tropical conditions.

Minimum tank size: LiveAquaria recommends 30 gallons (114 L), ReefApp 150 L, SaltwaterAquariumBlog 20 gallons (76 L). The computed average across sources is approximately 113 L; the lower figures may be acceptable for a single specimen but are generally considered too small for a group.

Salinity: LiveAquaria lists 1.020–1.025 SG; ReefApp references ~1.026 SG; SaltwaterAquariumBlog cites 1.025 SG. Averaged to 1.022–1.025 SG.

Adults reach approximately 7 cm (about 2¾ inches) maximum, consistent across hobbyist and scientific sources. Lifespan of 4–6 years has been reported for well-maintained specimens.

The species is demersal and oviparous; males have been documented guarding and aerating egg clutches and become notably territorial during spawning. Captive breeding has been documented but no established hobbyist protocol is widely available.
breedingNotes: Has been documented breeding in captivity. Males tend to be slightly larger and more slender; during spawning, males are generally described as highly territorial while guarding demersal egg clutches attached to substrate surfaces. Eggs have been reported to hatch in approximately 5 days. No widely established captive-breeding protocol exists for home aquariums.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been documented spawning in captivity; males guard demersal egg clutches and become highly territorial. Eggs reportedly hatch in approximately 5 days. No established home-aquarium breeding protocol is widely documented.
